replies.author: @a7HKdAbmET
replies.text:
Fwiw I liked the movies to a great degree, and some changes I'm fine with (changing tone of Aragorn's arc, Elves at Helm's Deep (there could be better ways to do the same effect, however), cutting of Tom Bombadil...). Some are questionable, but not done maliciously either way. What I'd like you all to consider is that Tolkien had a certain way of writing books in terms of pacing that would ruin any movie, even a trilogy. It might have made sense for an episodic thing (and with a grain of salt), with a full runtime of few dozen hours, but with what we got (and Jackson really did his best to steal every single extra minute of runtime), I believe most changes regarding pace and difference in dramatic tone were done rather well. 
Even some rather unexpected/unwelcome changes like introduction of Arwen in Glorfindel's stead were done in a rather tasteful manner, especially when you consider other franchises that were ruined with DEI or investment milking approach for maximum audience appeal. You could see the love and sweat invested into those movies, and the respect for source material was evident.
After all is said and done, it was an adaptation. The movies would have been horrible if they were following the books word-by-word anyways. Pacing would be off, bunch of things would be constantly slowing the plot down, you'd have too many characters introduced as important and heavy on details, only to be around for a minute or so, and never to be seen again... You can't please everyone, and there will always be valid critiques for the adaptation (notably the way Denethor, Boromir and Faramir were depicted, Arwen vs Glorfindel and others), but one has to consider that the story itself, in its original form, is deserving of some critique if it were to be adapted to a screen "as it is". 

Regarding Hobbit, that was something completely different and I really dislike those movies. It could be easily seen how they wanted to milk the story for all its worth, and given its limited scope, they expanded it as much as they could, and it was done with no respect to source material.

⁠@jimvac77I’ll add that I disagree with your points, but I also feel the film medium couldn’t take delays.

We get a 2,500 year synopsis followed by a 60 years later followed by Frodo enveloping the Ring. Film wise, it’s a bad idea add a third “17 years later” caption. Aragon isn’t reluctant in the books, but it’s also supplemental to the Glorfindel change, like Bombadil. He made a romantic subplot more paramount than ONE TIME APPEARANCE characters in the worldbuilding. If anything, these OP characters were meant to SHOW that within the coming pages, even having a level 10 Elf if your party doesn’t mean you will be buffed against a Balrog.

There’s more development with Aragorn in the movies than the film. Strider proudly pronounces the remade sword constantly in the books. He’s one a mission. But the films show a steady recognition of duty. If anything, the films are a more profound portrayal of one who acts because of duty and striving for upholding birthrights, rather than one of those which leads to the birthright being fulfilled.

These things don’t override the overall themes of Tolkien in the films, but again,some always get too specific to point out changes

My surname is Black, which originally meant someone with light skin and black hair. I’m from the black clan of Ireland. But on my father’s side my last name is farm in Denmark. Mostly Irish, but with a Danish last name

The “light” version you’re referring to got construed because of Christianity. it comes from Old English ælf (plural ylfe), which in turn comes from Proto Germanic albiz.  Earliest Origins: “White Being” or “Shining One”

“Shining one” easily interpreted as light. In the old pre-Christian worldview, elves (ælfar, álfar) were already described as luminous, shining, or white spirits beings connected to nature, ancestors, or divine light in a neutral or sacred sense. Their glow symbolized vitality, beauty, and otherworldly power, not holiness or moral purity.

And the easterlings, as I said before. The Easterlings were men from the far east of Middle-earth, beyond Mordor and the Sea of Rhun. They were not all one people but rather a collection of eastern tribes and kingdoms. In Tolkien’s stories, they’re often seen as dark-skinned, dark-haired, and more “eastern” in appearance, especially compared to the fairer Men of the West like Gondor and Rohan. There’s your black characters. 

But no no nooo we have to have a black elf and make him a star!  Idiotic. And insulting. And then make a black dwarf who emasculates everyone just like warrior Galadriel. Especially where women are hardly even present in the books. They have to have that intersexual feminism. 

The whole things a big joke. It’s deliberate to make a point, and get under our skin. bastardizing Anglo Saxon culture and ethnic history.

As someone with Celtic and Anglo-Saxon ancestry, Im Danish and Irish, I find it disheartening and insulting when adaptations of Tolkien’s world ignore the very cultures that inspired it. Tolkien created Middle-earth as a mythology for the British Isles, rooted in the languages, legends, and values of Northern Europe.

When they change those cultural foundations, it feels like they’re erasing a part of that heritage and replacing it with something that doesn’t belong to the original mythos. To me, that’s not about race, it’s about respecting the history, symbolism, and identity that Tolkien built into his work. To me it’s personal.

"How is the Dictionary getting on?' said Winston, raising his voice to overcome the noise.

'Slowly,' said Syme. 'I'm on the adjectives. It's fascinating.'

He had brightened up immediately at the mention of Newspeak. He pushed his pannikin aside, took up his hunk of bread in one delicate hand and his cheese in the other, and leaned across the table so as to be able to speak without shouting.

'The Eleventh Edition is the definitive edition,' he said. 'We're getting the language into its final shape -- the shape it's going to have when nobody speaks anything else. When we've finished with it, people like you will have to learn it all over again. You think, I dare say, that our chief job is inventing new words. But not a bit of it! We're destroying words -- scores of them, hundreds of them, every day. We're cutting the language down to the bone. The Eleventh Edition won't contain a single word that will become obsolete before the year 2050.'

He bit hungrily into his bread and swallowed a couple of mouthfuls, then continued speaking, with a sort of pedant's passion. His thin dark face had become animated, his eyes had lost their mocking expression and grown almost dreamy.

'It's a beautiful thing, the destruction of words. Of course the great wastage is in the verbs and adjectives, but there are hundreds of nouns that can be got rid of as well. It isn't only the synonyms; there are also the antonyms. After all, what justification is there for a word which is simply the opposite of some other word? A word contains its opposite in itself. Take "good", for instance. If you have a word like "good", what need is there for a word like "bad"? "Ungood" will do just as well -- better, because it's an exact opposite, which the other is not. Or again, if you want a stronger version of "good", what sense is there in having a whole string of vague useless words like "excellent" and "splendid" and all the rest of them? "Plusgood" covers the meaning, or "doubleplusgood" if you want something stronger still. Of course we use those forms already. but in the final version of Newspeak there'll be nothing else. In the end the whole notion of goodness and badness will be covered by only six words -- in reality, only one word. Don't you see the beauty of that, Winston? It was B.B.'s idea originally, of course,' he added as an afterthought.

A sort of vapid eagerness flitted across Winston's face at the mention of Big Brother. Nevertheless Syme immediately detected a certain lack of enthusiasm.

'You haven't a real appreciation of Newspeak, Winston,' he said almost sadly. 'Even when you write it you're still thinking in Oldspeak. I've read some of those pieces that you write in The Times occasionally. They're good enough, but they're translations. In your heart you'd prefer to stick to Oldspeak, with all its vagueness and its useless shades of meaning. You don't grasp the beauty of the destruction of words. Do you know that Newspeak is the only language in the world whose vocabulary gets smaller every year?'

Winston did know that, of course. He smiled, sympathetically he hoped, not trusting himself to speak. Syme bit off another fragment of the dark-coloured bread, chewed it briefly, and went on:

'Don't you see that the whole aim of Newspeak is to narrow the range of thought? In the end we shall make thoughtcrime literally impossible, because there will be no words in which to express it. Every concept that can ever be needed, will be expressed by exactly one word, with its meaning rigidly defined and all its subsidiary meanings rubbed out and forgotten. Already, in the Eleventh Edition, we're not far from that point. But the process will still be continuing long after you and I are dead. Every year fewer and fewer words, and the range of consciousness always a little smaller. Even now, of course, there's no reason or excuse for committing thoughtcrime. It's merely a question of self-discipline, reality-control. But in the end there won't be any need even for that. The Revolution will be complete when the language is perfect. Newspeak is Ingsoc and Ingsoc is Newspeak,' he added with a sort of mystical satisfaction. 'Has it ever occurred to you, Winston, that by the year 2050, at the very latest, not a single human being will be alive who could understand such a conversation as we are having now?'"

Orwell 1984

replies.author: @rikhuravidansker
replies.text: @simonh6371 The Matter of Britain is NOT English, indeed Tolkien said it was not English. The only Arthurian folklore that is considered English are those associated with specific places: i..e King Cole and "The Three Heads in the Well" for Colchester, Joyous Gard for Northumbria, and Ludgate for London. I did not mention Dummonian or Cumbric (historical Cumberland including the Lake District) folklore as they are strictly speaking not ethnically English per se in the same way the former Danelaw is not ethnically English.

replies.author: @simonh6371
replies.text: @rikhuravidansker Of course King Arthur is cherished as part of our English folklore.  As is Vortigern for whose existence there is more evidence, Caracatus, Boudicca, Caedmon the Cowherd, and many more...whom you've probably never heard of.  No idea what you mean by the ''Matter of Britain''.  Also classing Ludgate, an actual place in London, as Arturian folklore is a bit confusing.  Myself I am from Shropshire which was assimilated into Anglo-Saxon Mercia later than most of England and we have a strong consciousness of our joint Brittonic/Anglo-Saxon heritage.  My village was already settled when the Romans came so it long predates Germanic migration and we regard the failed attempts by the Danes to expand into our county as recent events, and the Danes/Norse as newcomers with their funny named villages like Quatt. As to Cumbric folklore, I am aware of this and the Brittonic Cumbric language which survived long after the Anglo-Saxons arrived.  It is not Welsh or Scottish so whether you agree or not it's part of English heritage (rather than folklore).

replies.author: @rikhuravidansker
replies.text: @simonh6371 I was raised in Shropshire for five-and-a-half years, nearly all eight-year olds know who Boudicca and Caractacus (Chief Caradoc of the Causellvaunii, High King of the Britons - having becoming chief and king after the death of his brother Tasciovantus - who ruled at Trinovantum or Caer Lhudd (London) and ancestor of King Arthur) are, and western Shropshire was settled by Welshmen in the Industrial Revolution (and no doubt was always Welsh). Eastern Shropshire was Wrekenscyrig whose capital was Wrekensbyrig (now the Wrekin). You call me ignorant but you do not know what the Matter of Britain is or that Ludgate was supposed to have been founded by High King Lhudd, an earlier ancestor of King Arthur (it was Lhudd who imprisoned the Red and White Dragons and who built Trinovantum or Caer Lhudd: the ravens of the Tower are supposed to be a reference to him). Tolkien said Arthurian legend was Strictly British, not English.

replies.author: @simonh6371
replies.text:
@rikhuravidansker Ah well if you talk of King Ludd then that's correct, he is mythological like Arthur as it's not certain whether he existed or not.  You only mentioned the present gate in London.  For gate to exist it has to be in something - a wall.  The Romans built the walls around Londinium (now the City) and the gate was named after him. 
I too was born and raised in fair Salop and have moved back to southern Shropshire now.  I'm well aware of the Welsh influence - there are places with Welsh placenames such as Clun, and Oswestry has always had Welsh speakers.  
Parts of Shropshire were absorbed into Mercia later on and remained Brittonic until the 7th or 8th century I think, as was Cumbria. Another one that fascinates me is the Brittonic kingdom of Pengwern, about which very little is known but it was almost certainly in Shropshire.
There's a Welsh speaking nursery (for kids, not plants) there. I agree that Arturian legend was British i.e. of the Britons and against the Anglo-Saxons.

replies.author: @rikhuravidansker
replies.text: @simonh6371 I should have mentioned Gog and Magog are part of London folklore, and that since the Old Forest is visualized on the area of Herefordshire and Staffordshire that became the county of West Midlands, and the Four Farthings are visualized on Staffordshire, Herefordshire, Oxfordshire, and Wiltshire (not in that order) Buckland would be visualized on Gloucestershire and Tookland on Shropshire. Tolkien said West Midlands English was the purest form of English (since Staffordshire, Herefordshire, Wiltshire, and Gloucestershire are usually considered part of southern England and the western half of the West Midlands was Welsh the language Tolkien was speaking of was the Shropshire accent, which is considered accentless in the same sense as Midwestern American English outside the Upper Midwest, Greater Chicago, and the General American region). Shropshire was the southernmost of Danelaw Mercia but therefore less subject to Danish settlement.

Part of the point is that Middle-earth is a new world, a false world, and yet is also just the real world. So as to display Christian principles in an unfamiliar world that lets JRR display how these principles are universally valid and applicable. Things like mercy and forbearance are core teachings of Christ that apply as fully to a hobbit and a wizard as they do to a regular layman in Briton or a child in Africa and they would have applied to cavemen and they will apply to men on Mars in 3025.

author: @captbuckyohare
text:
Just to offer a short set of points regarding the course at the University of Nottingham: 

The course posits that British literature (citing Shakespeare, Tolkien and C.S Lewis - born in Belfast btw - specifically) has contributed to a culture of British myth-making, and says it offers a critical analysis of how these authors may have contributed to a “fictional, mono-ethnic English past” and how their works reflect “ethnic chauvinism” and “anti-African antipathy”. It doesn't explicitly call the authors' works "racist", but it uses academic word salad synonyms instead.

The issues with the course are, as follows:

- It focuses on the service-level skin colour of selected characters or groups and projects modern racial political meaning selectively onto the literary text. Particularly with orcs (focussing on black skinned orcs only, the Easterlings, and Haradrim) and posits Tolkien's work contains harmful racially monolithic views, which cast certain groups as "evil" based purely on their geography. It's highly selective, myopic, and excludes wider descriptions of races (such as the non-black skinned orcs) which don't align with the course's conclusions.

- It ignores the racial and moral complexity of Tolkien’s other characters and races (e.g., Saruman, Sauron, Wormtongue, Denethor, Boromir, Gollum—all presented as white males, and all morally compromised at one point or another in the story, or simply out and out villains). These characters demonstrate that moral failure and evil are not racially exclusive in Tolkien’s legendarium. In fact, Tolkien’s portrayal of evil often critiques hubris, domination, and the loss of natural harmony, rather than racial identity. Their omission greatly undermines the credibility of the course and its author's understanding of the source text.

- It projects modern racial categories and political issues onto a mythological framework that Tolkien explicitly designed to reflect pre-modern, mythic Europe, not contemporary Europe / Britain.

- There is no mention of African or Asian fantasy traditions, mythologies, or literary works that present alternative models of race, geography, or morality that could provide a broader or more balanced framework for understanding racial representation in fantasy. This is either because the course author hasn't checked or researched such literature, or they have, and no appropriate counter examples exist. Many mythologies and fantasy traditions, regardless of origin, tend to centre the home culture as virtuous or chosen, and depict outsiders as threats, invaders, or morally suspect. It is a trope that is not unique to western literature. This omission is also pretty damaging to the course's credibility.

- The course also chooses not to direct or encourage students to explore any potential global mythologies and fantasy traditions on their own to help contrast Western literary tropes with non-Western ones, nor does it encourage literary appreciation alongside critique, rather framing the canon solely as a problem to be dismantled. It comes across as almost "anti-literary" in its content and intent.

- Instead, the course appears to focus narrowly on deconstructing British fantasy through a modern, predeterminate racial lens, without offering students the tools to build or explore alternative literary worlds, or appreciate the works while also fairly critiquing them. Which doesn't seem to offer much in the way of value or arm students with skills they could use to improve or appreciate literature from different eras, cultures, or different sensibilities. It seems to fall into the classic trap of bad academia: telling students what to think instead of teaching them how to think.

So yea. Totally the sort of course you're glad they charge students huge amounts of money to participate in. Go Nottingham!

replies.author: @captbuckyohare
replies.text:
@Justtalkingbricks That said - if they were inclined to look, there are examples of non-western literature you could cite... it just wouldn't help the course's point.

Full disclosure, I needed chatgopt's help with these so take them with a grain of salt but...:

African Epics and Fantasy
Sundiata: An Epic of Old Mali - Heroic homeland: Sundiata, the rightful heir to the Mali Empire, is portrayed as the destined savior of his people. Outsiders as threats: The sorcerer king Soumaoro Kanté, who opposes Sundiata, is depicted as a tyrant whose defeat restores moral and political order. Moral binaries: The epic reinforces the idea of divine destiny and cultural superiority of Mali over its enemies.

Emperor Shaka the Great - Zulu nationalism: Shaka is framed as a near-mythic figure whose military and political genius elevates the Zulu nation. Foreign threats: External tribes and colonial forces are depicted as destabilizing influences. Moral clarity: The narrative celebrates Shaka’s consolidation of power as a righteous act of cultural preservation.

Asian Fantasy and Mythology
Journey to the West: Moral geography: The journey from China to India is framed as a spiritual quest, but foreign lands are often depicted as chaotic or dangerous.
Cultural superiority: The monk Xuanzang and his companions represent Confucian and Buddhist virtues triumphing over barbarism. Binary tropes: Demons and monsters are frequently coded as “other,” reinforcing a moral divide between the civilizing mission and the wild.


Middle Eastern and Islamic Fantasy
The City of Brass: Islamic folklore: The magical city of Daevabad is a center of power and culture, while outsiders threaten its stability. Political allegory: The story explores internal divisions, but still frames the native magical elite as rightful rulers. Moral binaries: The djinn are divided into factions, with some coded as noble and others as corrupt or violent. [bestbookstoread.info]

One Thousand and One Nights
Cultural centrality: The tales often portray Arab or Persian protagonists as clever, virtuous, and favored by fate. Outsiders as exotic or dangerous: Foreign lands and peoples are frequently depicted as mysterious, threatening, or morally ambiguous. Heroic homeland: The stories reinforce the idea of the caliphate or kingdom as the seat of justice and wisdom


There's actually a really interesting subject to be explored here regarding literary writing, global points of view, and common tropes and devises that emerge regardless of the home culture of the author and how they manifest differently across different parts of the world. But this course isn't interested in that.

replies.author: @AnonymousNoNayme
replies.text:
Dr. Onyeka Nubia's "credentials" (you're gonna notice some patterns herepretty quick lol)...

Education: PhD in History (institution and date not publicly specified; research began post-undergraduate studies around 1993–1994).

He supposedly conducted "extensive archival research", analyzing over 250,000 historical documents on Africans in Tudor England (press X to doubt).

Professional Positions and Affiliations:

Assistant Professor of History, University of Nottingham, Department of History, Faculty of Arts (current); teaches modules like Imagining 'Britain': Decolonising Tolkien et al. and Conflict and Coexistence in Early Modern Europe.

Lecturer/Visiting Scholar at Edge Hill University, University of Huddersfield, University of London, Brunel University, London South Bank University, Queen Mary University of London, Birkbeck (University of London), University of Westminster, and SOAS (over 25 years).

Law Lecturer at various institutions, including University of London affiliates, providing legal advice in "deprived" UK areas (e.g., Tottenham, Peckham, Deptford, Brixton).

Director of Studies, Narrative Eye (his educational consultancy); runs Black history, creative writing, and personal development courses in community settings.

Fellow of the Royal Historical Society (FRHistS), recognizing contributions to historical scholarship.

Publications:

England’s Other Countrymen: Black Tudor Society (Zed Books, 2019) – Examines Black presence in Tudor England.

Blackamoores: Africans in Tudor England, their Presence, Status and Origins (Narrative Eye, 2013; revised 2014) – Pioneering archival study of Africans in 16th-century England.

The Phoenix (2009, novel under pen name Onyeka) – Won African Achievers Award for Communication and Media.

Young Othello (play) – Explores African kingdoms and human themes pre-colonialism.

Over 50 articles in UK historical magazines, including BBC History Magazine (e.g., "The Missing Tudors: Black People in Sixteenth-Century England," 2013).

Articles/chapters include "The Language of Racism" (2019) and "Africans in Early Modern England" (2019).

Awards and Recognitions:

National Teaching Fellowship (NTF), Advance HE (UK) (a charity and professional membership scheme), for contributions to teaching and diversity in higher education.

African Achievers Award for Communication and Media (2009) for The Phoenix.

Media and Consulting Work:

Historian/Presenter on TV programs: Digging for Britain (BBC), Walking Victorian Britain (Channel 5), History Cold Case: The Ipswich Man (BBC), Skeletons of the Mary Rose (Channel 4), Crossrail Discovery: London’s Lost Graveyard (Channel 4), Secrets of the Mary Rose (Channel 4), Shoot the Messenger (VoxAfrica, 2009), and Royal Autopsy (various episodes).

Featured in films like Ma’afa and African diaspora documentaries.

Public Speaking and Community Engagement:

Delivered keynote lectures at Houses of Parliament (UK), National Gallery (UK), National Portrait Gallery of Scotland, Oxford University, SOAS, Vanderbilt University, Georgia State University, Clark Atlanta University, Gresham College, St John's College Oxford, and University of York.

Topics include Black Tudors, decolonizing history, inequality, and British multiculturalism; gave Black History Month lecture (2025) on "Understanding England's Past."

25+ years running education programs in deprived UK areas; first Black British author to address "far-right rise and link self-hatred to violence"🙄.

Spoke at Detroit Public Schools African Child Symposiums (2002).

Additional Notes:

Supposedly faced academic skepticism (warranted by the looks of it) and "far-right threats" (again press X to doubt) in the 1990s, which allegedly drove his independent research on Black British history.

*So in other words he is a career DEI hire who specializes in...black people in the UK...* 🙄
